questionText: A 12V generator battery reads 12.6V at rest but collapses to 7.8V during crank, and the engine barely turns. Cable drop tests show under 0.2V per segment. The verdict is:

choices.text: A failed battery — it cannot deliver cranking current despite a healthy resting voltage

explanation: Resting voltage is not capacity. Collapse under load with clean cable drops isolates the battery itself — the load test the annual PM should have run.

questionText: A standby generator's batteries died although the engine-driven alternator tests perfectly. The component whose quiet failure explains this is:

choices.text: The station float charger — the engine runs too few hours to maintain the battery

explanation: A standby engine runs a couple of hours monthly; the float charger works around the clock. Its silent death is the classic dead-battery root cause.

questionText: The most preventable cause of a generator failing to respond to a real outage is:

choices.text: The controller left out of AUTO after the last service visit

explanation: Not-in-auto leads the industry's embarrassment statistics. The departure ritual — verify AUTO, breakers, valves, charger, heater, documented on the ticket — exists to make it impossible.

questionText: A generator outputs a rock-steady 480V but at 56 Hz. The fault territory is:

choices.text: The engine/governor — 56 Hz means the engine is running at 1680 RPM instead of 1800

explanation: Frequency IS engine speed on a synchronous alternator. Correct voltage clears the excitation side; hunt fuel delivery, governor, or overload for the missing RPM.

questionText: A generator trips on low oil pressure. The dipstick is full. Before any restart you must:

choices.text: Verify actual pressure with a mechanical gauge — senders lie, and bearings do not survive being wrong
Jumper the switch for a test run

explanation: The $20 sender is the most-replaced part on generator engines, but only the mechanical-gauge verification separates a lying sensor from an engine about to destroy itself. Never restart on hope.

questionText: A propane home-standby unit starts fine in summer but stalls under load during winter cold snaps. The root cause to evaluate is:

choices.text: LP tank vaporization capacity — cold slows boil-off and full load outruns the vapor supply

explanation: Vapor-withdrawal LP depends on tank heat; cold plus full demand collapses the supply exactly when the generator is needed. Tank sizing (or additional tanks) is the engineering fix.

questionText: A generator in a sound-attenuated enclosure overheats only at full load on hot days; the radiator, coolant, belts, and thermostat all check perfect. The generator-specific suspect is:

choices.text: Enclosure airflow: blocked louvers, failed damper actuators, or discharge air recirculating to intake

explanation: The enclosure is half the cooling system. Full-load-hot-day-only overheats with a clean engine point at the box: louvers, damper proving, and recirculation.

questionText: A generator that passed years of 30-minute monthly tests dies six hours into a hurricane outage with filters packed in black slime. The mechanism and the missing program were:

choices.text: Diesel bug and water at the tank bottom, stirred and drawn down by the long run; bottom-sampling and fuel polishing
Injector coking; more frequent oil changes

explanation: Short tests sip clean top fuel while the bottom builds sludge for years. The real outage stirs and draws deep. Bottom sampling, testing, and polishing programs exist because of exactly this story.
referenceBookSection: Module 17 — Diesel Fuel Systems, Storage & Quality

questionText: A diesel generator starts hard, hunts under load, and dies a few minutes after every run — with no fuel leak visible anywhere. The classic cause is:

choices.text: A suction-side air leak: air enters where fuel does not exit

explanation: Vacuum-side leaks admit air invisibly — no drip ever forms. Foam in cracked lines or clear-hose inspection finds the corrupted delivery behind the hunting and stalling.
referenceBookSection: Module 17 — Diesel Fuel Systems, Storage & Quality

questionText: A diesel exercised weekly at no load for years weeps black oily residue from its exhaust joints and smokes heavily when finally loaded. The condition and its cure are:

choices.text: Turbo seal failure; replace the turbo
Wet stacking from chronic light loading; sustained load-bank burn-off runs and a corrected exercise program

explanation: Slobber is unburned fuel and soot from an engine never worked hot. The cure is the work it was denied — loaded burn-off — and exercise at meaningful load thereafter.
referenceBookSection: Module 17 — Diesel Fuel Systems, Storage & Quality

questionText: A generator holds exactly 60.0 Hz but produces only 350V on a 480V system, and AVR adjustment does nothing. The classic fault is:

choices.text: Failed rotating rectifier diodes starving the main field

explanation: Perfect frequency clears the engine; low, unresponsive voltage points down the excitation chain, where the shaft-mounted diode set is the classic failure — testable with the set stopped.

questionText: During a large block load, voltage dips well below setpoint exactly as frequency sags to 57.5 Hz, and both recover together seconds later. This behavior is:

choices.text: Under-frequency roll-off: the AVR deliberately reducing voltage so the lugging engine can recover

explanation: V/Hz protection trades voltage for engine recovery during load steps. Voltage tracking frequency down and back together is the signature of the feature working.

questionText: A generator cranks, clearly fires and runs — but the starter keeps grinding into the running engine until overcrank lockout. The classic fault is:

choices.text: The magnetic pickup or its wiring: the controller never saw disconnect speed

explanation: Crank disconnect depends on the speed signal. A dead pickup leaves the controller blind to the running engine — the grinding overlap is its signature.

questionText: A customer reports their generator 'shut down randomly last Tuesday but runs fine now.' Your first diagnostic act is:

choices.text: Reading the controller event log: the timestamped trip and its surrounding parameters

explanation: The controller witnessed everything — which trip, what temperatures and pressures were doing, for how long. The log converts the ghost into a specific, diagnosable event.

questionText: Working inside an ATS cabinet during a utility outage, the required assumption is:

choices.text: The cabinet is dead — the utility is out
Both sources are potentially live: the generator side is hot or may start automatically — LOTO both sources and verify every section
Only the load lugs are dangerous

explanation: The outage that killed utility just made the generator side live. Two-source LOTO with verification testing of every section is the ATS cabinet's defining discipline.

questionText: During an outage the generator ran perfectly at its pad, but the building stayed dark. Generator voltage and frequency measured at the ATS sensing terminals are correct. The fault territory is:

choices.text: The ATS itself: acceptability settings, transfer timer, controller, or mechanism

explanation: Good power delivered to a switch that never moved indicts the decision-maker. Sensing setpoints, timers, and the mechanism — under full two-source safety discipline.

questionText: A facility's generator has been flawless for years, but during the real outage the never-transfer-tested ATS mechanism failed to move. The lesson is:

choices.text: The un-exercised transfer mechanism is the system's single point of failure — periodic transfer testing is as essential as engine exercise
Closed transition should have been used

explanation: Mechanisms bind when never moved. A perfect generator behind a frozen switch delivers nothing — the argument for real transfer testing programs.

questionText: A sync-check relay refuses to permit a generator breaker closure although panel meters 'look close.' The professional response is:

choices.text: Jumper the relay — meters are close enough
Trust the permissive: verify voltage, slip, and phase matching and diagnose why the window is not met — out-of-sync closure shears couplings
Close manually with the breaker handle
Trip the bus and use dead-bus closure

explanation: The sync check guards against the most violent mistake in the power world. Eyeball-close is not in-window: diagnose the synchronizer inputs, never bypass the guard.

questionText: Two equally rated paralleled generators show unit A at 80% kW and unit B loafing at 20%, with bus voltage and frequency normal. The investigation belongs at:

choices.text: The governors/load-sharing controls — kW division on a shared bus is a fuel/governor function

explanation: On a shared bus, each machine's kW share follows its governor and load-share settings; excitation problems would show as kVAR imbalance instead. The paralleled division of labor.

questionText: A single generator feeds three ATSs serving egress lighting, stairwell pressurization, and a data floor. Their NEC classifications are most likely:

choices.text: Article 700 (egress), 701 (pressurization), 702 (data floor) — one plant, three rule sets

explanation: Life-safety egress = 700 (10-second transfer); legally required hazard systems = 701; the business's data floor = 702. The class on each switch sets its rules and priorities.
referenceBookSection: Module 22 — Generators in Critical Power Systems

questionText: A data center UPS cycles endlessly between generator power and battery during outages; the generator panel reads 'normal.' The productive investigation is:

choices.text: Record generator frequency/voltage through the events and compare against the UPS input-window settings — the handshake is the fault, not either machine alone

explanation: The classic generator-UPS friction: transient wobble outside a tight input tolerance. Instrument recordings plus both event logs decide which side gets tuned.
referenceBookSection: Module 22 — Generators in Critical Power Systems

questionText: A hospital generator designated Level 1, Type 10, Class 48 must:

choices.text: Serve life-safety stakes, accept load within 10 seconds, and carry fuel for at least 48 hours of runtime
Be one of 48 units on site

explanation: The three-word shorthand: Level = stakes, Type = seconds to load, Class = fuel hours. It dictates the readiness chain, the testing calendar, and the tank.

questionText: A generator shuts down on high coolant temperature at minute 25 of its monthly load test. The professional framing to the facility manager is:

choices.text: The test just caught a real cooling failure safely, with help present — instead of at hour 22 of a real outage; now we diagnose
Tests should be shortened to 15 minutes
Skip next month's test as a precaution

explanation: Tests fail systems on your terms. The alternative discovery scenario is mid-hurricane. This framing turns the awkward moment into the case for honest testing.

questionText: To an AHJ auditor, a monthly generator test that was performed but never documented is:

choices.text: A test that never happened — the written record IS the compliance
Covered by the controller's internal log

explanation: Compliance is the documented program: readings, dates, signatures. Controller logs support but never replace the record the audit inspects.

questionText: An event log shows the start signal arriving, three healthy crank cycles at good battery voltage, then overcrank lockout — the engine never fired. The tree branch to walk is:

choices.text: Fire: fuel delivery (solenoid, day tank, filters, suction air), then air and compression — with the block heater behind cold-start versions

explanation: The log already cleared command and crank. Strong cranking with no fire is the diesel trinity, with the fuel path leading the suspects.

questionText: Both generators at a site died within an hour of each other during an extended outage, filters slimed black. The shared-cause investigation targets:

choices.text: The common bulk fuel supply — tank-bottom water and diesel bug feeding both engines the same sludge

explanation: Simultaneous victims share causes. One contaminated tank serves both engines the same failure on the same schedule — the classic multi-set outage story.

questionText: After a generator failed during a real outage at a hospital, and before any alarm clearing or repair, you must:

choices.text: Restart it as fast as possible
Export/preserve the event logs and document found conditions — outage failures at Level 1 sites face insurance and regulatory review

explanation: The preserved record protects every party — including you and the hospital. Clearing alarms first destroys the witness statement the review will demand.

questionText: A customer asks how long their 1,000-gallon tank runs a 300 kW generator at full load. The field estimate is roughly:
explanation: ≈7 gal/hr per 100 kW → ~21 gal/hr at 300 kW → 1,000 ÷ 21 ≈ 47-48 hours. Runtime math turns tank gallons into honest emergency-planning numbers (and NFPA Class ratings).

questionText: Working on generator starting batteries requires the same discipline as UPS battery strings because:

choices.text: They present the same hazards: high fault current across tools, acid, hydrogen gas, and weight

explanation: A wrench across battery terminals welds itself identically in a generator room or a UPS room: insulated tools, PPE, no metal jewelry, ventilation awareness — the shared battery modules exist for both trades.
referenceBookSection: Modules 11-12 — Battery Types & Safety (shared with UPS)

questionText: The generator trade's recognized industry certification ladder is run by:

choices.text: EGSA — the Electrical Generating Systems Association's Apprentice and Journeyman Technician certifications

explanation: EGSA is the trade's credential body — the generator analog of NATE (HVAC) and CFESA (kitchen), with manufacturer schools providing warranty authorization alongside.
referenceBookSection: Module 25 — Career in Power Generation Service
